HLD learning path

Distributed systems building blocks

Go deeper on coordination, partitioning, probabilistic techniques, and workflow patterns that appear repeatedly in the primer and in senior-level interviews.

Module progress 0%

0 of 5 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Consistent hashing and hot-key management

Partitioning strategy determines whether scale-out stays smooth or turns into painful rebalancing and overloaded shards.

Why this module matters Build one clean mental model before scaling it.

Move through the lessons in order so each trade-off has context before the harder distributed systems decisions show up.

Study target 5 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

High-level design interview prep 5 lessons 0/5 complete Follow in order for best results Back to HLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the summary, inspect the diagram, and identify the sharpest trade-off.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
HLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Explain the building blocks behind partitioning, coordination, and large-scale data movement
  • Recognize when to use quorums, hashing, probabilistic structures, and sagas
  • Connect these lower-level concepts back to product-facing system designs

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

0/5 complete
Lesson 1 Next
Consistent hashing and hot-key management

Partitioning strategy determines whether scale-out stays smooth or turns into painful rebalancing and overloaded shards.

30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 2 Open
Consensus, quorums, and leader election

Some systems need a clear write owner or coordinated control plane, and that requires more than just adding replicas.

30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 3 Open
Distributed transactions, sagas, and idempotent workflows

Once one user action touches multiple services, the real design problem becomes how to preserve intent when part of the workflow fails halfway through.

30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 4 Open
Probabilistic data structures and cardinality estimation

Bloom filters, sketches, and approximate counters let large systems answer useful questions cheaply when exact answers would be too slow or too expensive.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 5 Open
Batch processing, stream processing, and MapReduce

Not every workload belongs on the synchronous request path; many large systems depend on layered data pipelines for indexing, analytics, backfills, and asynchronous enrichment.

30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Consistent hashing and hot-key management

Open

Partitioning strategy determines whether scale-out stays smooth or turns into painful rebalancing and overloaded shards.

Why it matters

Many system design answers mention sharding, but stronger candidates can explain how data moves during capacity changes and how to survive skewed traffic.

What you will cover

Why consistent hashing existsHot partitions still need extra controlsTie hashing to operational behavior

Good interview signal

  • Explain why modulo sharding causes painful remaps.
  • Mention virtual nodes or tokens for balance.
30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Consensus, quorums, and leader election

Open

Some systems need a clear write owner or coordinated control plane, and that requires more than just adding replicas.

Why it matters

Interviewers often probe replication and failover depth by asking who is allowed to write, how failover is detected, and what happens during split-brain risk.

What you will cover

Quorums trade latency for coordination safetyLeader election is about ownershipUse coordination sparingly

Good interview signal

  • Name who owns writes and how that ownership changes.
  • Explain how replicas detect and recover from leader failure.
30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Distributed transactions, sagas, and idempotent workflows

Open

Once one user action touches multiple services, the real design problem becomes how to preserve intent when part of the workflow fails halfway through.

Why it matters

This is where shallow microservice answers usually break down: interviewers want to know how money, inventory, reservations, or emails stay coherent under retries and partial failure.

What you will cover

Understand transaction boundariesSagas model long-running business intentIdempotency closes the retry loop

Good interview signal

  • Name the local transaction boundary for each step.
  • Explain how the workflow recovers from a mid-flight failure.
30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 4

Probabilistic data structures and cardinality estimation

Open

Bloom filters, sketches, and approximate counters let large systems answer useful questions cheaply when exact answers would be too slow or too expensive.

Why it matters

These are classic primer topics and strong interview differentiators because they show you can trade tiny error bounds for major savings in memory, storage, or request cost.

What you will cover

Use approximation where exactness is wastefulPlace probabilistic structures carefullyExplain the operational trade-off

Good interview signal

  • State what kind of approximation error is acceptable.
  • Use approximate structures as filters or summaries, not silent truth replacement.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 5

Batch processing, stream processing, and MapReduce

Open

Not every workload belongs on the synchronous request path; many large systems depend on layered data pipelines for indexing, analytics, backfills, and asynchronous enrichment.

Why it matters

The primer covers MapReduce and asynchronous data movement because scalable systems often separate online serving from offline or near-real-time computation.

What you will cover

Choose the right processing modeMapReduce is a mental model for data-parallel workOperationalize the pipeline

Good interview signal

  • Separate online serving from offline or streaming computation.
  • Choose batch or stream based on freshness and cost requirements.
30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
